note: Two distinct weathered tenement frontages with partial signage ('...Of Leith Walk', 'Adams Whyte Cri...') and a clean red-brick building with columned dark shopfront, all reading as coherent if grimy street-level buildings.
Wide street view with legible shop signage ('Foot Of Leith Walk', 'Shug McGubbin', 'Subway') across varied, coherent tenement facades.
Camera is pressed right up against the wall, filling the frame with a blown-up, blurry fragment of masonry and two window bays with no ground floor or shop entrance visible at all.
Row of tenements across the street with a legible pub sign and coherent brick/stone facades, partially occluded by two voxel NPC figures in the foreground.
A stone tenement with readable 'Edinburgh Carer...' signage beside a clean pale building with a dark colonnaded shopfront, both reading as real buildings.
Multi-building street row with legible 'Edinburgh Carer...' signage and varied, distinct facades; no repeated tiling or stretching visible.

Camera is again jammed against the wall, showing a huge blurry close-up of cracked stonework and window fragments with no ground floor, plus a shop sign ('Deni Pakwan') visible only at a sharp raking angle on an adjacent flower-draped wall.
Street corner with readable 'Desi Pakwan' and 'Papa Joh[n's]' shop signage on distinct, coherent building facades.
Classic red-columned shopfront entrance with dark interior and no visible signage, next to a building with legible 'VAS MOTORS' and '...D WORKS' signs; reads as real derelict shopfronts.
Busy street row with multiple legible shop signs ('The Pipe Shop', 'VAS MOTORS', 'Cameron's Sweet Pa...', 'Carpet Bargain Store', 'Sloe Lane Bake...') on varied, coherent facades.
Camera is jammed almost inside an oversized painted sign reading blurred, pixelated letters ('eith Ma...') that fill the entire frame with no shopfront structure visible.

A red-brick two-storey building in the middle of the row appears flatly cropped at a hard rectangular edge and awkwardly overlapping/clipping into the taller sandstone tenement behind it, not meeting at a natural building line.
Close-up row of shopfronts ('LEITH DEPOT' with striped awning, 'ELLA TASTE OF GRE...') reads clearly as coherent, legible real shop frontages.
Wide street view with legible shop names (Zoomo, Kiki's Cafe, Cornelius) along a varied row of tenement facades; a parked van partially blocks one shopfront but nothing reads as broken or stretched.
Extreme close-up on a red doorway shows heavily blurred, pixelated wood grain and flat solid-black window panes with no interior detail, clearly beyond the texture's native resolution.
Two distinct tenement facades (dark grimy one with a striped awning reading '...chkeith House - P...', and a clean cream sandstone one) look coherent, though a van blocks most ground-floor detail.
Close-up shopfront signage is reduced to illegible blurred fragments reading only 'Ta' and 'Es', not a coherent shop name.
While 'Majes...' and 'Bandits' read fine, the shopfront in the middle of the row is an illegible blurred fragment ('as. Ta. Ea') that doesn't cohere into a real shop name.
Camera looks down a gap between buildings into flat, untextured grey slab side-walls with sparse dark window-shaped smudges and no real facade detail, breaking the photographic illusion.
Foreground shopfronts (Millers Flooring, Urban Jungle) read fine, but the same flat, untextured grey gap walls are visible in the background between the rows, breaking the illusion.
Ground floor shows a legible 'Arkay Imaging' shopfront with a striped awning and an adjacent partially-framed sign, weathered but coherent upper-storey stonework above.
A row of distinct, readable shopfronts (FRITES, Arkay Imaging, Volunteer Edinburgh, Ladbrokes) sits under varied period facades that look like a real derelict Edinburgh terrace.
Camera is pressed almost against the wall producing heavy out-of-focus blur, leaving only a fragment of a sign reading 'ALHAMB...' with the actual shopfront below indistinguishable.
Curved corner block with several legible shopfronts (William Hi..way, The Phone Stop, La Belle Advent..., Alhambra Ba...) reads as a plausible derelict Edinburgh street corner.
Two adjacent, clearly legible shopfront signs ('...ST Kebab House' and 'The Fairy Boy Of Leith') sit beneath scorched stonework that reads as a real fire-damaged tenement.
The sign band reads 'ARTISAN COFFEE LEST KEBAB HOUSE', two different shop names visibly overlapping/collapsed into one illegible run of text on the same frontage.
Camera is jammed extremely close to the facade, rendering it as an out-of-focus blur where only a fragment of a sign ('Sea Breeze Ca...') is legible and the actual shopfront geometry cannot be seen.
A run of clearly readable, distinct shopfronts (Dixy Chicken, Lindsay & Gilmour, Sea Breeze Cafe, Ahmad An... Kebabi) under varied weathered facades looks like a real strip of Leith Walk businesses.
Legible shopfront signs ('...harp Style Barber' and 'GOLDIE...') flank a plain grey gable-end wall in a narrow gap between buildings; the wall itself is blank rather than faulty.

A row of legible shopfronts ('[Neig]hbourwood', 'Style Barber', 'GOLDIE LOXA...', 'GRIFFEN FITNESS') sits under coherent period stonework, obscured partly by construction fencing/cones in the foreground.
Camera appears to be clipped inside/against the wall geometry, showing only an extreme close-up of blurred sign lettering and a dark metal shutter grille with no coherent shopfront visible.
A row of derelict sandstone tenements with legible shop signage (Balfour Street, +48 Tattoo, Indo So'Arthur's, Your Move) reads convincingly as a real, if ruined, Edinburgh street.
Most of the frame is dominated by a flat, blank grey wall with faint unexplained vertical shadow marks and no window or shopfront detail, breaking the illusion immediately.
The same untextured flat grey building (with faint vertical smudges, no windows or ground-floor detail) sits centrally in the row between otherwise detailed tenements.
Camera is clipped extremely close to a wall/sign surface, showing only blurred oversized lettering fragments ('HANG', 'Bro') with no readable shopfront.

Two adjacent building elevations (around 'Braid Vets' and the neighbouring unit) show near-identical damaged-window masonry texture, reading as an obvious copy-pasted repeat rather than distinct buildings.
Close-up on legible striped-awning shopfronts ('Alterations & Dry Cleaning', 'Kukina') with weathered stonework and broken windows above reads as a plausible derelict shop row.

A long street row of distinct, legibly-signed shopfronts (Amor, Alterations & Dry Cleaning, Kukina, Taste of Poland, First Mortgage, St Columba's) with varied tenement facades reads convincingly as a real street.
A large flat, blank grey facade with faint unexplained vertical marks and no windows or shop detail sits prominently mid-frame between two textured, detailed buildings.

A rounded red-brick corner building ('Epoca') with legible neighbouring shopfronts (Yellow Express, Tech) and a derelict street scene with an abandoned car reads convincingly, aside from a smaller blank grey facade visible further down the block.
A shuttered ground-floor unit reading 'STARLIGHT' with a striped awning sits below three clean, regularly-spaced sash windows, entirely plausible as a real derelict shopfront.
A full three-storey stone tenement is visible with legible 'STARLIGHT' and 'Scotmid' shopfront signage and evenly proportioned sash windows above, reading as a coherent real building.

The camera is jammed almost against a dark glass shopfront so the frame is nearly all featureless black glazing with only a fragment of a sign reading '...s Furniture' cropped at the top, giving no coherent sense of what shop this is.

Two brick pillar-like sections flanking a recessed facade show an identical arched window-bay tile stacked repeatedly like wallpaper, and there is a visible mirrored/ghosted seam artifact between building sections.
The entire ground floor is a plain featureless dark shutter/wall with no legible signage or shop identity at all, only ordinary windows visible above it.

Starbucks, Sainsbury's, Pret A Manger and Marpole shopfronts are legible, but the brown building on the left shows an obviously repeated grid of identical window-bay tiles stacked like wallpaper across its upper storeys.

Viewed down the street at a steep raking angle, the nearest building's shopfront signage ('WORD...') is visibly warped and stretched sideways in a way that looks like a texture-mapping artifact rather than natural perspective.
A row of tenement buildings shows legible signage including 'EDINBURGH MORTGAGE', '...ighton', 'VORD OF', 'OLDEN AMBAL' and 'sponge', reading as a believable row of derelict high-street shops.
Above the 'Grace Church Leith' shopfront, the window band shows an obvious mirrored/reflected duplicate strip of the same windows, a clear texture artifact rather than authored detail.

The same mirrored/duplicated window-row artifact seen at close range is visible again across the white building's facade even from further away, alongside legible 'Grace Church Leith' and 'PLACES LEISURE' signage.
Camera is jammed against a blurry sign fragment reading only "...ORLD" with a dark void below and no coherent shopfront visible.

A darker sepia-toned facade texture is overlaid at an offset on top of a lighter facade behind it, creating a hard rectangular seam that cuts through the building mid-elevation with mismatched brick tone and misaligned windows either side.
Readable ground-floor signage ("MADEB..." awning and "British Red Cr[oss]") with plausible masonry above, no stretching or repetition visible.
A row of legible shopfronts (MADEB, British Re..., The Walnut Salon, Borlands, Nails & S...) reads as a normal derelict tenement row.
Camera is pressed almost flat against the wall, producing an out-of-focus mass of indistinct shapes with only a fragment of the letters "...E" legible; no shopfront reads as coherent.
Clean row of legible shopfronts (Leith Army Stores Outdoors, Not Just Coffee, Tesco Express) with a parked car and plausible derelict upper storeys.
Readable signage (VIDEO, HADDINGTON PLACE, INDIGO) with masonry above that looks like ordinary adjoining Georgian tenement facades.
A long readable shopfront row (YOGA, Settlement Projects, WEX PHOTO VIDEO, HADDINGTON PLACE, INDIGO SUN) with varied but plausible upper-storey facades.
Extreme close-up against the wall leaves only blurry fragments "MAX" and "OH D..." visible with no coherent shopfront readable.
Clearly readable row of shops (OH DEER, TWELVE TRIANGLES, ADEEL, Mcdonald Road) with a plain undetailed gable-end wall between buildings that reads as a party wall, not a fault.
Two ground-floor shop signs overlap illegibly, rendering as garbled text like 'LITTLE PLA/Dental' instead of one coherent shopfront.
From further back the same overlapping/merged shop signage is still visible, breaking the otherwise plausible derelict tenement row.
Camera is pressed almost flush against a roller shutter, producing a heavily blurred close-up where only a text fragment ('SEND') is legible.
Adjacent shopfront signs bleed into each other (e.g. 'SMART'/'RAVEL'/'Bike Control' overlapping), producing illegible composite text.

A large plain grey gable end wall (plausible as a fire-damaged neighbouring building) fills the left of frame, while the corner shopfront on the right reads clearly enough ('...Central', 'Edinburgh...').
A large flat grey building side shows faint garbled ghost lettering ('ARTH...') and blocky rectangular shading patches that read as a broken/low-res texture rather than real masonry or signage.
Camera is pressed up against a sign, producing an extremely blurred close-up showing only the fragment word 'Phone'.
Neighbouring shop signs overlap and merge (e.g. 'SHINE MY NAIL'/'Workwear Direct', 'Phone Spolair'), producing illegible combined text.
One building's upper floors show an obviously tiled/mirrored window-bay pattern repeated with no distinguishable ground-floor shopfront.
The central dark-brown building displays a clearly repeating vertical grid of identical window bays, reading as wallpapered texture rather than an authored facade.
The central building's upper floors show an obvious mirrored/symmetric window pattern with a visible vertical seam down the middle, reading as a duplicated texture rather than a real facade.
A building on the right shows a strong diagonal chevron/diamond zigzag pattern across its upper storeys that looks like a tiled or mismapped texture rather than real masonry.
Extreme grazing angle down the row limits detail, but the visible 'Fitshop' signage reads as a real shopfront and no clear stretching, cropping or repetition is evident.
A row of distinct, legible shopfronts (Kahani, Down the Hatch, Fitshop) with plausible weathered brick upper floors and no visible repetition or distortion.
The Ladbrokes shopfront sits beside a visibly skewed, trapezoidal panel of window texture, and the building corner is built from angled flat panels that don't align into a coherent 3D volume.
The same chevron/diamond-patterned building from the close pose is visible at the left edge, and the corner building further along appears to bulge/lean unnaturally.
